utterances: Well, thank you very much.
We were speaking with Melissa Harris-Lacewell, associate professor of politics and African-American studies at Princeton University, and Chief John Timoney, Miami's chief of police.
Jose Garces is among the most talented and innovative chefs in America.
He opened his first restaurant, Amada, in 2005, and since then, his Garces Group has opened 14 other restaurants across the country.
He won the James Beard Award for Best Chef in the Mid-Atlantic region in 2009, and he's also a Food Network Iron Chef, along with the likes of Bobby Flay, Cat Cora and Michael Symon.
Now Chef Garces has a new book, ""The Latin Road Home."" Part cookbook, part memoir and travelogue, Garces leads readers on a delicious journey through five distinct cultures and cuisines.
Chef Garces will join us in a moment, but we also want to hear from you.
Tell us about one food or meal, which instantly transports you home.
Our email address is talk@npr.org.
And you can join the conversation at our website.
Go to npr.org, and click on TALK OF THE NATION.
Chef Jose Garces is in the studios of member station WHYY in Philadelphia.
So good to have you with us.
Welcome to TALK OF THE NATION.
It's a pleasure to be here.
Thank you.
Now this book, ""The Latin Road Home,"" focuses on the cuisine of five different countries: Ecuador, Spain, Cuba, Mexico and Peru.
First of all, why those five countries?
What is it that they have in common that you wanted to explore?
Well, they're all countries that have been inspirational to me throughout my 20 years of cooking.
And they all have their own kind of unique elements that have inspired my cooking.
And, you know, we could start with the first one, Ecuador.
I'm Ecuadorian.
I was born and raised in Chicago, but both of my parents emigrated from Ecuador.
And then, I mean, really, I could tell a story about each country and why it's particularly...

You know what I was going to try and do with you, if you wanted to play this game, kind of an ""Iron Chef"" kind of game, which was just sort of go through each of the country and give me a quick, little thumbnail sketch, maybe even just word that's - one word that might be - really will describe, you know, something about that country's food.
Let's start with Ecuador since you were talking about it.
Oh I would say Indian native.
And that means what exactly?
That just mean - these indigenous people that have been there and have, you know, have their traditions in food have been translated throughout the generations and are still ever present.
Spain.
Spain.
Well, you know, for me, I think Spanish food is festive.
It's European.
It's classic, and it's involved in all of the cuisines that I spoke of.
Mexico.
Mexico.
Ancient, traditional, really - gosh, the root of cuisine.
Peru.
Peru, I think has the same similar features that Ecuador has and their neighboring countries.
But just again, very indigenous, very rustic, home style.
And Cuba.
Cuba.
The country where my wife is from, so Fidel Castro and...
(LAUGHTER)
It is a cuisine that is Caribbean.
It's festive.
It's fun, but it has some roots.
You know, I like the fact that these are all linked in some way, and it's almost hard to trace the links.
You sort of open the book by saying that, you know, certain foods had gone back and forth across, you know, from Spain to - from the New World to the old, and then back again.
And I wanted you to talk a little a bit about that and how they're all kind of linked in some way.
Do they all begin in Spain, or...
I think that the Spanish domination throughout, you know, the - throughout time has - they've imprinted their cuisine, their culture in these different areas.
And then you take the indigenous population that was there during the Spanish colonization, and that marriage is really what was become their cuisine currently.
Yeah.
And I know so little about - you know, so interested that your heritage is Ecuadorian, if that's the correct way we're putting it...
Mm-hmm.
...because I don't know a thing about Ecuador.
I was thinking about it.
I thought, I don't know about food from Ecuador, what it is like.
(LAUGHTER)
Yeah.
Well, it's not a popular, I would say, mainstream cuisine here in the U.S.
It's something that, I think, still needs to be discovered, and it's a very flavorful cuisine.
I think, you know, one of the things that I've - some of the best cooks that I've cooked with, whether it's in New York or Chicago or even here in Philadelphia, are actually Ecuadorians.
And there's something about their palates that really just inspires me, and has a really deep sense of flavor.
Can you give me an example of a sort of, you know, classic Ecuadorian dish or meal?
Unidentified Woman #2: Some of them aren't even old enough to drink.
The story for Lisa and Ken O'Connell, who we met at the potluck, takes a different turn.
They learned not long ago that their daughter has two more kids, and she hadn't told them.
The toddlers were in a foster home in Ohio.
The O'Connell's say they had no choice.
Those kids are now living with them too in central Massachusetts.
Their daughter hasn't spoken with them since.
For NPR News, I'm Jill Kaufman.
Let's turn now to education, where policymakers from President Obama on down say it is the job of schools to prepare students for college, or for the world of work.
If that's the job, do parents think schools are doing well?
In a recent survey by NPR, the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation and the Harvard School of Public Health, most parents said yes, with one exception - a big exception.
Many said schools did not sufficiently prepare young women for the world of work.
NPR's Claudio Sanchez has this report.
Kyrah Whatley, 17, is a bright student with pretty good grades.
But the thought of spending two to four more years in a college classroom is depressing.
Masonry, on the other hand, intrigues her.
I like it just because it's like building stuff, and I'm a kinesthetic learner.
I learn with my hands.
So I'm going into the Navy; like, I can be a mason.
That's right.
Kyrah is thinking of joining the Navy as a certified mason, right after she graduates from Buchtell High in Akron, Ohio; and no one is more supportive than her masonry teacher, Matt Simpson.
Kyrah, where's my 90-degree square?
Today, Simpson is having Kyrah and her classmates work on a mock-up foundation for a house they're building.
I need everybody on this side so we all know what we're doing.

Inside the huge, cavernous warehouse that's both a classroom and a storage facility for building materials - lumber, bricks - and heavy equipment, there are no desks; just long tables covered in chalk-like dust.
A cheerful man in his early 30s, Simpson is a product of Akron's public schools.
He skipped college; went on to make six figures as a mason, and a contractor, before he started teaching at Buchtel.
So he agrees with Kyrah: College isn't for everybody.
When I was in school, that same thing was beaten into me too also - you had to go to college, you had to go to college.
So I needed to figure out, if I'm not going to go to college, I'm not also going to work at McDonalds.
So what am I going to do?
Students today face the same question, and it's especially frustrating for young women, says Simpson.
They face more hurdles, both real and perceived.
Women, after all, are still a tiny minority in good-paying trade like plumbing, welding and masonry.
Kyrah says people even tried to talk her out of enrolling in the masonry program.
Some thought learning how to lay brick was crazy.
But I said it doesn't matter 'cause any women can do anything as a man can do.
But who tried to talk you out of it?
My mother, a couple of friends, and then they was like, that's a boys' class.
I was like, I don't care.
Kyrah says Buchtel High has given her what she needs to do well after high school.

But when NPR, the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation and Harvard School of Public Health asked parents in a recent survey if schools were sufficiently preparing students who don't go to college for the world of work, parents of girls were more likely to say no than parents of boys.
Christianne Corbett is not surprised.
She's a researcher at the American Association of University Women, where she studies gender equity in education and the workplace.
In this economy she says, girls and boys need more than a high school diploma.
We've seen, over time, more and more jobs do require some sort of higher education; and just a high school degree really is not enough for individuals to support themselves and their families.
And women are paid less than men at every level of education.
So when you take that into consideration, women with a high school degree don't fair even as well as men with just a high school degree.
Huh.
High schools, as we know them, are already obsolete.
